heroTitle: Understand the questions family asks all the time.
heroText: This lesson helps English-dominant learners recognize common Tamil home questions and answer them with short, natural replies.
panelList: How to understand frequent family questions
How to answer briefly without translating word by word
How to sound natural in casual home conversations
title: Common family questions and replies
description: This lesson focuses on questions relatives and parents ask constantly: where you are going, where you were, when you will come back, and what you did today. The goal is not perfect grammar. The goal is being able to keep the conversation moving.

audio.note: This localhost lesson is wired for ElevenLabs-generated audio. Configure ELEVENLABS_API_KEY and ELEVENLABS_VOICE_ID in .env.local, then run serve.py.

phrases.useCase: A very common question from parents or siblings when you are about to leave.
phrases.note: Short, direct, and common in spoken Tamil at home.

phrases.meaning: I'm going to a friend's house.
phrases.useCase: An easy natural reply to say where you are headed.
phrases.note: Code-mixing with words like 'friend' is common in many Chennai homes.

phrases.useCase: A common question when you come back after being out.
phrases.note: This is one of the most useful pattern-recognition questions for learners.

phrases.useCase: A quick answer when someone asks where you were.
phrases.note: You can swap 'office' for other locations once the pattern becomes familiar.

phrases.useCase: A common end-of-day question from parents or relatives.
phrases.note: Learners will hear this a lot in home conversations.

phrases.useCase: A very common short answer when you do not want to explain much.
phrases.note: This is high-frequency and makes learners sound immediately more natural.

phrases.useCase: Used when someone wants to know when you will return home.
phrases.note: Understanding time questions is especially useful for home conversations.

phrases.useCase: A simple answer when you do not want to give an exact time.
phrases.note: This is a very practical reply for beginners.

phrases.useCase: Useful when you want to manage expectations without a long explanation.
phrases.note: This is a very realistic mixed-language phrase for urban Tamil speech.

phrases.useCase: Useful when someone is asking you to talk or do something immediately.
phrases.note: This helps learners manage a conversation without sounding rude.

phrases.useCase: A polite follow-up when you are busy or want to postpone a conversation.
phrases.note: This is better than a blunt no, and very useful in real family interactions.

phrases.useCase: Use when someone asks you what happened or what you are planning.
phrases.note: A strong bridge phrase that keeps the conversation going.

dialogues.context: You are heading out and want to answer simply and naturally.
dialogues.explanation: This directly answers the question and sounds like a normal casual home reply.

dialogues.context: You don't know the exact time, but you want to reassure them.
dialogues.explanation: This is a natural reassuring answer when you do not want to give a precise time.

dialogues.context: You do not want to explain much. What is the best short reply?
dialogues.explanation: This is a very common short answer and works well in family conversations.

dialogues.prompt: You are busy and someone wants to talk right now.
dialogues.context: Which answer sounds the softest and most natural?
dialogues.explanation: This politely delays the conversation instead of sounding abrupt.

recalls.note: One of the most common family questions you will hear.

recalls.prompt: Say: 'I'm going to a friend's house.'
recalls.note: A useful simple location reply for beginners.
You will hear this often in home conversations.
A soft way to postpone a conversation.
